name: Nvidia
overview: Nvidia is the dominant supplier of AI accelerators, founded in 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem, and headquartered in Santa Clara, California. Originally a PC graphics company (the GeForce line), it pivoted GPUs into general-purpose compute with CUDA in 2006 and is now the most valuable semiconductor company in the world, having crossed multi-trillion-dollar market capitalization. Its flagship data-center products are the Hopper generation (H100, H200) and the Blackwell generation (B100/B200 and the GB200 Grace Blackwell superchip), sold inside DGX systems and the rack-scale GB200 NVL72. Nvidia is fabless, manufacturing on TSMC and packaging with CoWoS, and pairs its silicon with NVLink/NVSwitch interconnect, ConnectX/BlueField networking from its Mellanox acquisition, and Spectrum-X/Quantum InfiniBand fabrics.
strategy: Nvidia sells full-stack AI infrastructure rather than bare chips: GPUs, NVLink/InfiniBand networking, DGX and HGX systems, and a deep software moat in CUDA, cuDNN, TensorRT, Triton, and the enterprise AI Enterprise suite. It monetizes through hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Meta), neoclouds (CoreWeave, Lambda), enterprises, and sovereign-AI national programs, capturing the lion's share of training and a large share of inference spend. The company deliberately keeps annual cadence high to stay ahead of buyers' depreciation cycles and competitors. It positions itself as the platform for accelerated computing broadly, not just AI, spanning Omniverse, autonomous vehicles (DRIVE), and robotics (Jetson/Isaac).
differentiation: The genuine moat is CUDA plus the surrounding library ecosystem accumulated over nearly two decades, which makes porting workloads to rivals expensive and risky. Nvidia uniquely ships rack-scale systems where NVLink turns 72 Blackwell GPUs into a single coherent compute domain (GB200 NVL72), an interconnect advantage competitors using PCIe or Ethernet cannot easily match. Owning Mellanox gives it the networking layer that determines real cluster throughput, so it sells the whole datacenter fabric, not just the compute node. Its software releases continually extract more performance from installed hardware, lengthening the value of each generation.
roadmap: Nvidia has committed to a roughly annual product cadence: Blackwell Ultra (GB300) followed by the Rubin architecture and Vera CPU, with Rubin Ultra and the Feynman generation signposted further out. It is pushing rack-scale and even multi-rack coherent designs, co-packaged optics to scale interconnect bandwidth, and continued investment in inference-optimized variants as the workload mix shifts from training to serving. Strategic bets include sovereign AI deployments, enterprise agentic AI, physical AI/robotics via Isaac and Omniverse, and automotive. It is also deepening networking with Spectrum-X Ethernet to court customers who resist InfiniBand.
rationale: Nvidia's thesis is that AI is a new computing platform and that whoever owns the developer ecosystem and the full system stack captures the value, not whoever has the fastest single chip. It chose general-purpose programmable GPUs plus CUDA over fixed-function ASICs because flexibility let it ride every wave from gaming to crypto to deep learning to transformers without redesigning silicon for each. It invested early and heavily in software when that looked unprofitable, betting that lock-in and performance-per-watt at the rack level would compound. Buying Mellanox reflected the conviction that at scale the network, not the GPU, becomes the bottleneck.
references:
Key people: Jensen Huang (co-founder, CEO), Chris Malachowsky, Curtis Priem (co-founders), Bill Dally (chief scientist)
Flagship products: H100/H200 (Hopper), B200/GB200 (Blackwell), GB200 NVL72 rack, CUDA, NVLink/NVSwitch, ConnectX/BlueField
https://www.nvidia.com/
https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-zone
https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/gb200-nvl72/

name: AMD
overview: Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is a fabless semiconductor company founded in 1969, headquartered in Santa Clara, California, and led since 2014 by CEO Lisa Su. It competes across CPUs (EPYC server and Ryzen client), GPUs (Radeon and the data-center Instinct line), and adaptive computing (FPGAs and adaptive SoCs from its 2022 Xilinx acquisition). Its flagship AI accelerators are the Instinct MI300 family — the MI300X GPU and MI300A APU — followed by the MI325X and the MI350/MI355X series, built on the CDNA architecture and manufactured by TSMC with advanced 2.5D/3D chiplet packaging. AMD also acquired ZT Systems to build rack-scale AI systems and bolster its data-center systems engineering.
strategy: AMD positions itself as the credible open alternative to Nvidia, leading with high memory capacity and bandwidth, competitive total cost of ownership, and an open software stack (ROCm) versus proprietary CUDA. It monetizes through hyperscalers and large model builders — Microsoft Azure, Meta, Oracle, and others have deployed MI300X — plus enterprise and sovereign customers, while EPYC server CPUs fund and complement the GPU push. Its go-to-market emphasizes drop-in inference and the ability to fit large models on fewer chips thanks to large HBM. AMD also leans on its CPU leadership in data centers to land-and-expand into accelerators.
differentiation: AMD's MI300X ships with substantially more HBM memory (192GB on MI300X) than the comparable Nvidia part of its generation, which lets a single GPU hold larger models and reduces the chips needed for inference. Its chiplet packaging expertise — pioneered in EPYC and Ryzen — lets it combine CPU and GPU dies in a unified APU (MI300A) with shared memory, a design used in the El Capitan exascale supercomputer. The Xilinx acquisition gives AMD adaptive silicon and networking IP (including Pensando DPUs) that few competitors have under one roof. It champions open standards like UALink and Ultra Ethernet against Nvidia's proprietary NVLink and InfiniBand.
roadmap: AMD has laid out an annual Instinct cadence: MI325X, then the CDNA 4 MI350/MI355X series, followed by the next-generation MI400 built for the rack-scale Helios system co-developed with partners. It is investing heavily in ROCm software maturity, day-one model support, and developer tooling to close the gap with CUDA. Strategically it is moving from selling chips to selling rack-scale systems (via ZT Systems) and backing open interconnect consortia (UALink, Ultra Ethernet Consortium) to commoditize the fabric. It continues to integrate networking and CPU strengths to offer a full data-center alternative.
rationale: AMD's thesis is that customers want a second source and an open ecosystem to avoid single-vendor lock-in, and that openness plus aggressive memory and TCO can pry open a market Nvidia dominates. It chose chiplets early because disaggregating large dies improves yield and lets it mix CPU, GPU, and I/O dies flexibly — the same bet that revived its CPU business now powers its accelerators. It backs open interconnects rather than building a proprietary fabric because, as the challenger, commoditizing the network undermines a key part of the incumbent's moat. Prioritizing memory capacity reflects a read that inference economics increasingly hinge on fitting models in fewer GPUs.
references:
Key people: Lisa Su (CEO), Mark Papermaster (CTO), Forrest Norrod (data-center lead)
Flagship products: Instinct MI300X/MI300A, MI325X, MI350/MI355X, EPYC CPUs, ROCm, CDNA architecture
https://www.amd.com/en/products/accelerators/instinct.html
https://www.amd.com/en/products/software/rocm.html
https://www.amd.com/

name: Cerebras Systems
overview: Cerebras Systems is an AI compute company founded in 2015 by Andrew Feldman, Gary Lauterbach, and others (several from the team behind SeaMicro), headquartered in Sunnyvale, California. Its defining product is the Wafer-Scale Engine (WSE), the largest chip ever built — a single processor occupying nearly an entire 300mm wafer, with the WSE-3 carrying roughly 900,000 AI cores and 4 trillion transistors. These wafers ship inside the CS-3 system, and clusters of CS-3s form the Condor Galaxy supercomputers built in partnership with G42. Cerebras also runs a high-speed inference cloud and has filed to go public.
strategy: Cerebras sells both on-premises wafer-scale systems and a cloud inference/training service, targeting customers who need extreme speed or who want an alternative to GPU clusters without the complexity of distributed training. Its commercial momentum has been heavily anchored by a large relationship with G42 in the UAE, which has driven a substantial share of revenue and funded the Condor Galaxy build-outs. It increasingly markets fast inference — serving large models like Llama at very high tokens-per-second — as a differentiated cloud product. It positions wafer-scale as simplifying the programming model: one giant chip instead of thousands of GPUs to orchestrate.
differentiation: By keeping an entire model's compute on one wafer with enormous on-chip SRAM and on-wafer interconnect, Cerebras avoids the off-chip memory and inter-GPU networking bottlenecks that limit GPU clusters, enabling extremely low-latency, high-throughput inference. The wafer-scale design collapses what would be thousands of discrete chips and their interconnect into a single device, simplifying parallelism so users avoid much of the distributed-systems engineering GPUs require. Its weight-streaming architecture decouples model storage (in external MemoryX) from compute, allowing very large models on a fixed number of systems. No competitor ships a commercially deployed wafer-scale processor.
roadmap: Cerebras is iterating the WSE line (WSE-3 on TSMC 5nm, with successors expected to follow process and architecture advances) and scaling its inference cloud to more models and higher throughput. It is expanding data-center capacity in the US and abroad to serve growing inference demand, and pursuing an IPO to fund that build-out. Strategically it is leaning into being the fastest inference provider for frontier open models and into sovereign/enterprise supercomputing deals beyond G42. Diversifying its customer base away from concentration risk is an explicit priority.
rationale: Cerebras's thesis is that AI's hardest scaling problem is communication — moving data between chips and memory — and that the cleanest solution is to make the chip as large as physically possible so more of the computation stays on-silicon. It chose wafer-scale, an approach the industry long deemed impractical due to yield and power-delivery challenges, betting it could solve defect tolerance with redundant cores and novel packaging/cooling. This trades manufacturing difficulty for a vastly simpler programming and scaling model, on the conviction that ease of use and raw speed would win latency-sensitive workloads. The inference pivot reflects a read that serving, not training, is where wafer-scale's latency edge is most monetizable.
references:
Key people: Andrew Feldman (co-founder, CEO), Gary Lauterbach (co-founder, CTO)
Flagship products: Wafer-Scale Engine (WSE-3), CS-3 system, Condor Galaxy supercomputers, Cerebras Inference cloud
https://www.cerebras.ai/
https://www.cerebras.ai/product-chip
https://www.cerebras.ai/inference

name: Groq
overview: Groq is an AI inference company founded in 2016 by Jonathan Ross, who previously helped originate Google's Tensor Processing Unit, and is headquartered in Mountain View, California. Its core hardware is the Language Processing Unit (LPU), a deterministic, software-scheduled processor purpose-built for low-latency inference of large language models rather than training. Groq delivers this primarily as GroqCloud, an API-based inference service, alongside on-premises GroqRack systems. The company is known for record-setting tokens-per-second speeds on open models and has signed a very large multi-year capacity agreement to deploy compute in Saudi Arabia with HUMAIN.
strategy: Groq monetizes inference as a service through GroqCloud, charging per token and competing on speed and price for serving open-weight models (Llama, Mixtral, and others), and also sells systems to enterprises and governments. It deliberately abstains from the training market, focusing the entire stack on fast, cheap inference where it believes margins and demand are heading. Its go-to-market leans on a generous developer platform and viral speed demos to build bottoms-up adoption, plus large sovereign and enterprise capacity deals. Notably, Groq's chips are fabricated on a mature node and avoid HBM and advanced packaging, which it uses as a cost and supply-resilience argument.
differentiation: The LPU is fully deterministic with no caches or reorder buffers — the compiler statically schedules every operation, eliminating runtime unpredictability and enabling extremely low, consistent latency that GPUs struggle to match for sequential token generation. It uses large on-chip SRAM instead of external HBM, sidestepping the memory-bandwidth bottleneck and the constrained, expensive HBM supply chain that gates GPU production. Because performance comes from architecture and a compiler rather than bleeding-edge process nodes, Groq can use older, cheaper, more available silicon. The result is a system optimized end-to-end for inference latency and throughput per dollar rather than general-purpose flexibility.
roadmap: Groq is scaling GroqCloud capacity aggressively, including the major HUMAIN/Saudi Arabia data-center commitment and additional global deployments, to meet surging inference demand. It is iterating next-generation LPU silicon to push performance-per-dollar further while preserving the SRAM-based, deterministic design. Strategically it is expanding model coverage on GroqCloud, courting enterprise and agentic workloads where low latency compounds across multi-step reasoning, and pursuing sovereign-AI partnerships. Continued investment in its compiler and software tooling is central, since the architecture's advantage depends on static scheduling.
rationale: Groq's thesis is that inference, not training, will be the overwhelming majority of AI compute spend, and that inference rewards a fundamentally different, simpler, deterministic architecture than the flexible GPUs built for training. Ross's TPU background informed the bet that compiler-driven static scheduling removes the hardware complexity GPUs carry for general workloads, yielding predictable low latency. Choosing on-chip SRAM over HBM was a deliberate move to dodge the memory-bandwidth wall and the HBM supply bottleneck that constrains and prices GPU output. Building on mature nodes reflects a conviction that supply availability and cost will matter as much as peak FLOPS as inference scales.
references:
Key people: Jonathan Ross (founder, CEO), Sunny Madra
Flagship products: Language Processing Unit (LPU), GroqCloud, GroqRack
https://groq.com/
https://groq.com/groqcloud/
https://wow.groq.com/why-groq/

name: SambaNova Systems
overview: SambaNova Systems is an AI hardware and platform company founded in 2017 by Rodrigo Liang (former Oracle/Sun executive) and Stanford professors Kunle Olukotun and Christopher Ré, headquartered in Palo Alto, California. Its silicon is the Reconfigurable Dataflow Unit (RDU) — most recently the SN40L — which uses a dataflow architecture instead of a fixed GPU pipeline, paired with a three-tier memory hierarchy that includes large DRAM for holding many or very large models. SambaNova sells this as the DataScale system and the SambaNova Suite, and offers SambaNova Cloud for fast inference. The company has been heavily venture-funded and counts national labs and enterprises among its customers.
strategy: SambaNova's go-to-market centers on selling complete, integrated AI platforms — hardware plus pretrained and fine-tunable models plus software — often as a managed, full-stack solution rather than raw chips, appealing to enterprises and governments that want turnkey deployment behind their firewall. It monetizes via system sales, a 'dataflow-as-a-service' subscription model, and increasingly a fast inference cloud serving large open models. It targets regulated industries, sovereign deployments, and national labs (such as work with US Department of Energy facilities) where on-premises control matters. Its pitch emphasizes serving very large models and many models on comparatively few sockets.
differentiation: The RDU's reconfigurable dataflow design maps a model's computation graph spatially onto the chip, avoiding the kernel-launch overhead and rigidity of GPUs and allowing operator fusion across an entire model. Its standout feature is a large addressable memory tier (DRAM alongside on-chip and HBM), which lets a single node host hundreds of models or extremely large models and switch between them quickly — well-suited to agentic and multi-model workloads. This makes it efficient for serving large mixtures of experts and for enterprises running many specialized models. The full-stack, models-included delivery is itself a differentiator versus chip-only vendors.
roadmap: SambaNova is advancing its RDU line and SambaNova Cloud to serve ever-larger open models at high speed, leaning into inference as the growth vector. It is targeting agentic AI and multi-model serving as a natural fit for its large-memory, fast-model-switching architecture. Strategically it continues to pursue sovereign-AI and enterprise/government on-premises deals where data control is paramount. Deepening its software suite and model library to keep the turnkey value proposition strong remains a priority.
rationale: SambaNova's thesis, rooted in its founders' Stanford research on reconfigurable dataflow, is that mapping computation spatially is fundamentally more efficient than the instruction-driven, kernel-by-kernel GPU model, especially for large models and operator fusion. It chose a three-tier memory hierarchy with abundant DRAM because it bet that the future is many large models being served and swapped, not one model on one accelerator — a wager that anticipated agentic, multi-model deployments. Selling integrated systems with models included reflects a conviction that enterprises value turnkey outcomes over assembling their own stack. On-premises focus targets the segment Nvidia's cloud-heavy ecosystem serves less directly.
references:
Key people: Rodrigo Liang (co-founder, CEO), Kunle Olukotun (co-founder, chief technologist), Christopher Ré (co-founder)
Flagship products: Reconfigurable Dataflow Unit (SN40L RDU), DataScale, SambaNova Suite, SambaNova Cloud
https://sambanova.ai/
https://sambanova.ai/technology/sn40l-rdu-ai-chip
https://cloud.sambanova.ai/

name: Tenstorrent
overview: Tenstorrent is an AI and RISC-V compute company founded in 2016, headquartered in Toronto, Canada, and led since 2021 by CEO Jim Keller, the renowned chip architect behind designs at AMD (Zen), Apple, and Tesla. It builds AI accelerators based on its Tensix core architecture — product families including Grayskull, Wormhole, and the newer Blackhole — alongside high-performance RISC-V CPU IP (the Ascalon line). Tenstorrent sells discrete cards, Galaxy server systems, and licenses its IP, and has open-sourced large parts of its software stack. It has raised substantial funding and signed IP-licensing and co-development deals, including with partners pursuing custom AI and automotive silicon.
strategy: Tenstorrent pursues a dual model: selling its own AI hardware (cards and systems) and licensing its AI and RISC-V CPU IP to others building custom chips, an approach that lets it monetize even customers who want their own silicon. It emphasizes open standards and an open software stack to lower adoption friction and avoid the lock-in it argues defines incumbents. It targets cost-sensitive buyers, sovereign and national-champion chip programs, and companies wanting to co-design accelerators. Its networking-centric design — Ethernet-based chip-to-chip scaling baked into each processor — is a core part of the pitch for building large systems from commodity-friendly parts.
differentiation: Tenstorrent integrates RISC-V CPU cores directly alongside its Tensix AI cores and builds native Ethernet networking into every chip, so accelerators can be strung together into large fabrics without proprietary interconnect like NVLink. Its bet on RISC-V (an open instruction set) plus an open-sourced software stack is a deliberate openness play that contrasts sharply with CUDA's closed ecosystem. The Tensix core is designed for flexible scaling and high compute density at attractive cost, and the IP-licensing option means a customer can take Tenstorrent's architecture into their own bespoke chip. Having Jim Keller's architecture pedigree is itself a recruiting and credibility advantage few startups can claim.
roadmap: Tenstorrent is rolling out its Blackhole generation and advancing Ascalon RISC-V CPUs, with multi-generation roadmaps for both AI cores and CPU IP and plans for chiplet-based products combining them. It is scaling Galaxy systems and Ethernet-fabric clusters for larger deployments, and expanding IP-licensing engagements with sovereign and enterprise partners. Strategically it is doubling down on open-source software maturity to make its hardware genuinely usable as a CUDA alternative. Pursuing standard-cell chiplet ecosystems and broadening RISC-V adoption are explicit long-term bets.
rationale: Tenstorrent's thesis is that the industry needs an open alternative to closed, proprietary AI stacks, and that betting on RISC-V and open software will eventually erode the moat that lock-in provides incumbents. It builds Ethernet networking into every chip because it believes scaling should use open, commodity-friendly fabrics rather than a vendor-proprietary interconnect, lowering system cost. Offering IP licensing alongside products reflects a read that many large players and nations will want their own custom silicon, so selling the architecture is as valuable as selling the chip. Keller's team prioritizes architectural efficiency and openness over chasing peak benchmarks at any cost.
references:
Key people: Jim Keller (CEO), Ljubisa Bajic (founder), Keith Witek
Flagship products: Tensix core, Grayskull/Wormhole/Blackhole accelerators, Ascalon RISC-V CPU, Galaxy systems
https://tenstorrent.com/
https://tenstorrent.com/en/hardware
https://github.com/tenstorrent

name: Etched
overview: Etched is an AI chip startup founded in 2022 by Gavin Uberti, Chris Zhu, and Robert Wachen (Harvard dropouts), based in Cupertino, California. Its singular product is Sohu, an ASIC that burns the transformer architecture directly into silicon — a fully specialized inference chip rather than a general-purpose accelerator. By hard-wiring the transformer, Etched claims order-of-magnitude gains in throughput and cost-efficiency over GPUs for transformer inference. The company raised a large Series A (over $120 million) in 2024 to bring Sohu to market and has positioned it as a server delivering enormous tokens-per-second for large models.
strategy: Etched's go-to-market is to sell Sohu-based inference servers and capacity to companies serving large transformer models at scale, where its specialization promises dramatically lower cost-per-token than GPUs. It is a high-conviction, single-bet company: rather than hedging across architectures, it commits entirely to the transformer being the dominant model family for the foreseeable future. It targets the inference market specifically, where economics reward extreme specialization, and pitches that one Sohu server can replace many GPU servers. Its funding and narrative lean on the argument that AI labs are converging on transformers, making a transformer ASIC's risk acceptable.
differentiation: Sohu is genuinely a transformer ASIC — the attention and feed-forward operations are physically etched into the hardware, so it cannot run non-transformer models but runs transformers far faster and cheaper than any programmable GPU. Because it sheds all the general-purpose flexibility (and associated silicon and energy overhead) that GPUs carry, it can dedicate nearly the entire die to transformer math, claiming throughput on the order of magnitudes beyond contemporary GPUs for inference. This is the most extreme specialization in the field: where others build flexible accelerators, Etched bets the model architecture itself is stable enough to cast in silicon. The trade-off — zero flexibility — is the differentiation.
roadmap: Etched's near-term roadmap is bringing Sohu to volume production and standing up inference deployments, proving the cost-per-token claims at scale. Success would likely lead to subsequent generations on newer process nodes and support for the evolving transformer variants (including mixtures of experts and longer context) that the field adopts. Strategically the company is betting that as inference demand explodes, the market for a dedicated transformer chip grows, and it must keep pace with architectural tweaks that remain within the transformer family. Expanding software to map popular open and frontier transformer models onto Sohu efficiently is essential.
rationale: Etched's thesis is that the transformer has won — that nearly all frontier AI now uses the same architecture — and that once a workload stabilizes, the economically correct move is to build a fixed-function ASIC for it, just as crypto moved from GPUs to ASICs. It chose total specialization over the flexibility everyone else preserves because flexibility costs silicon area and power that, for a converged workload, is pure overhead. The bet is that the cost and speed advantage of hard-wiring will outweigh the risk that the architecture shifts away from transformers. This is a deliberate, contrarian wager that model-architecture convergence is permanent enough to justify casting it in silicon.
references:
Key people: Gavin Uberti (co-founder, CEO), Chris Zhu (co-founder), Robert Wachen (co-founder)
Flagship product: Sohu transformer ASIC
https://www.etched.com/
https://www.etched.com/announcing-etched

name: d-Matrix
overview: d-Matrix is an AI inference chip company founded in 2019 by Sid Sheth and Sudeep Bhoja, headquartered in Santa Clara, California. Its flagship is Corsair, a PCIe-form-factor inference accelerator built around a digital in-memory computing (DIMC) architecture combined with a chiplet design. d-Matrix targets generative-AI inference specifically, optimizing for serving large language models with high throughput, low latency, and strong energy efficiency. The company has raised significant funding (including a Series B led by Temasek and backing from Microsoft's venture arm) and brought Corsair to commercial availability.
strategy: d-Matrix sells inference accelerators and systems aimed at data-center operators and enterprises serving transformer models, competing on tokens-per-second-per-watt and cost rather than chasing training. It positions itself squarely in the inference market, which it argues is becoming the dominant and most cost-sensitive segment of AI compute. Its go-to-market emphasizes interactive, batched LLM serving where memory bandwidth and energy efficiency dominate the economics. Backing from strategic investors connected to major cloud and AI players signals a path into hyperscale and enterprise inference deployments.
differentiation: d-Matrix's core innovation is digital in-memory computing: performing matrix multiplications within or adjacent to the memory itself, attacking the memory-bandwidth wall (the 'memory wall') that bottlenecks transformer inference on conventional architectures. Unlike analog in-memory approaches that struggle with precision, its DIMC is digital, preserving accuracy while gaining efficiency. The chiplet-based Corsair design uses large amounts of fast on-package SRAM to keep model weights close to compute, dramatically reducing the energy and latency cost of moving data. This memory-centric specialization for inference, rather than general-purpose compute, is its defining edge.
roadmap: d-Matrix is scaling Corsair into production deployments and developing successor architectures (publicly signposted future products) that push in-memory compute and chiplet integration further. It is expanding its software stack and model coverage to make the platform easy to adopt for popular open and enterprise LLMs. Strategically it bets on the continued shift of AI spend toward inference and on energy efficiency becoming the decisive metric as power and cost constrain data centers. Deepening relationships with hyperscale and enterprise customers, supported by its strategic investors, is central to its growth plan.
rationale: d-Matrix's thesis is that the binding constraint on transformer inference is memory bandwidth and the energy spent shuttling weights, not raw arithmetic throughput, so the right architecture computes where the data lives. It chose digital in-memory computing over analog because digital preserves the numerical precision LLMs need while still collapsing the data-movement cost. It bet exclusively on inference because it judged that serving, not training, is where the volume, cost pressure, and efficiency demands concentrate. The chiplet plus large-SRAM approach reflects a conviction that keeping weights on-package is the cleanest way to break the memory wall economically.
references:
Key people: Sid Sheth (co-founder, CEO), Sudeep Bhoja (co-founder, CTO)
Flagship product: Corsair inference accelerator, digital in-memory computing (DIMC) architecture
https://www.d-matrix.ai/
https://www.d-matrix.ai/product/

name: Graphcore
overview: Graphcore is a British AI semiconductor company founded in 2016 by Nigel Toon and Simon Knowles, headquartered in Bristol, UK. It built the Intelligence Processing Unit (IPU), a massively parallel processor with large on-chip SRAM and a fine-grained MIMD architecture, delivered in the Bow IPU and IPU-POD/Bow Pod systems. After early hype and significant venture funding, Graphcore struggled commercially against Nvidia's dominance and CUDA's ecosystem, and in 2024 it was acquired by SoftBank, becoming part of SoftBank's broader AI infrastructure ambitions. Under SoftBank it operates as a UK-based AI compute developer with renewed backing.
strategy: Graphcore's original strategy was to sell IPU hardware and its Poplar software stack to AI researchers and enterprises as a differentiated alternative to GPUs, particularly for sparse and graph-structured workloads. Commercially it found the market hard to crack because of CUDA's entrenchment and the capital intensity of competing with Nvidia. Following the SoftBank acquisition, its direction is being reshaped within SoftBank's larger AI infrastructure agenda, which spans Arm, data centers, and ambitions to build full AI compute platforms. Its go-to-market is effectively being redefined under new ownership with deeper capital.
differentiation: The IPU's architecture put very large amounts of SRAM directly on-chip and used thousands of independent cores executing in a bulk-synchronous-parallel model, designed to excel at fine-grained, sparse, and irregular computation where GPUs are less efficient. Its Poplar software and graph-compiler approach exposed the processor's parallelism for machine-learning graphs. This made it genuinely novel architecturally — a from-scratch AI processor rather than a repurposed GPU. Its differentiation, however, was ultimately undercut by the practical reality that the broader ecosystem and largest models optimized for GPUs and CUDA.
roadmap: Under SoftBank ownership, Graphcore's roadmap is being integrated into SoftBank's wider AI infrastructure strategy, which includes leveraging Arm IP and building data-center-scale AI compute. Concrete next-generation IPU product plans are being recalibrated with new resources and strategic direction. The acquisition gives it the deep capital it previously lacked to attempt to compete at scale. Its future likely lies in contributing architecture and talent to SoftBank's ambition to be a vertically integrated AI compute provider rather than as an independent challenger.
rationale: Graphcore's founding thesis was that AI needed a purpose-built processor designed for the structure of machine-intelligence workloads — sparse, graph-like, fine-grained parallelism — rather than GPUs adapted from graphics. It chose massive on-chip memory and a MIMD many-core design because it believed keeping data on-chip and supporting irregular parallelism would beat GPUs on the workloads it expected to matter. The hard lesson was that architectural elegance could not overcome CUDA's ecosystem lock-in, the gravitational pull of GPU-optimized models, and the capital required to compete. The SoftBank acquisition reflects a pragmatic recognition that surviving in AI silicon now demands enormous capital and a full-stack platform strategy.
references:
Key people: Nigel Toon (co-founder, CEO), Simon Knowles (co-founder, CTO)
Flagship products: Intelligence Processing Unit (IPU), Bow IPU, IPU-POD/Bow Pod, Poplar SDK
https://www.graphcore.ai/
https://www.graphcore.ai/products/ipu

name: Broadcom
overview: Broadcom is a diversified semiconductor and infrastructure-software giant, headquartered in Palo Alto, California, and led by CEO Hock Tan; the modern company was formed when Avago Technologies acquired the original Broadcom Corporation in 2016 and took its name. It is a powerhouse in networking silicon (Tomahawk and Jericho switch chips), custom ASICs, RF and broadband components, and — after acquiring VMware in 2023 — enterprise infrastructure software. In AI, Broadcom is the leading partner for custom AI accelerators (XPUs), co-designing chips for hyperscalers including, most famously, Google's TPU, and supplying the networking that stitches AI clusters together. It is one of the most valuable semiconductor companies in the world.
strategy: Broadcom's AI strategy is to be the indispensable arms dealer behind the hyperscalers' own silicon: it provides the design expertise, IP (SerDes, packaging, networking), and manufacturing partnership for custom XPUs that companies like Google, Meta, and others build to reduce dependence on Nvidia. It monetizes through high-value custom-ASIC engagements and by dominating AI networking with its Ethernet switch portfolio (Tomahawk and Jericho). Hock Tan's playbook combines this with disciplined acquisitions and a large, sticky infrastructure-software business (now including VMware) that smooths revenue. It positions itself as the neutral enabler of every hyperscaler's in-house AI chip ambitions plus the connective fabric of AI data centers.
differentiation: Broadcom's edge is unmatched custom-silicon design capability paired with the best-in-class IP — its SerDes, advanced packaging, and chip-design methodology let hyperscalers ship competitive accelerators they could not build alone. It simultaneously owns the AI networking layer with Tomahawk and Jericho Ethernet switches, so it benefits whether the compute is Nvidia GPUs or custom XPUs, because the cluster still needs its fabric. This dual position — designing the accelerators and the network that connects them — makes it structurally exposed to AI growth without betting on a single architecture winning. Few companies combine leading networking silicon, custom-ASIC services, and a massive software business.
roadmap: Broadcom is scaling its custom AI accelerator business with multiple large hyperscaler customers and has signaled a very large multi-year revenue opportunity from XPUs and AI networking. It is advancing next-generation Tomahawk and Jericho switches, co-packaged optics, and scale-up/scale-out Ethernet fabrics to support ever-larger AI clusters, championing Ethernet over proprietary interconnect. It continues integrating and monetizing VMware to build out a full enterprise infrastructure stack. Strategically it bets that the number of hyperscalers and large AI players designing custom silicon will grow, expanding its custom-ASIC pipeline.
rationale: Broadcom's thesis is that it does not need to win the merchant-GPU war — it wins by enabling everyone who wants to escape Nvidia's pricing and own their silicon, while also selling the network that every AI cluster requires regardless of who makes the compute. It chose the custom-ASIC and networking position because it is architecture-agnostic and lower-risk: it profits from the overall growth of AI infrastructure rather than from any one chip design prevailing. Backing Ethernet for AI scaling reflects a conviction that open, commodity-friendly networking will displace proprietary fabrics as clusters grow. Hock Tan's disciplined, acquisition-driven model layers durable software revenue on top of cyclical silicon, a deliberate hedge.
references:
Key people: Hock Tan (CEO), Charlie Kawwas (Semiconductor Solutions president)
Flagship products: Tomahawk and Jericho Ethernet switch chips, custom AI XPUs (e.g. Google TPU partnership), VMware
